rewrites.newText: In Trafalgar Square, close to Nelson's Column
rewrites.targetsMisreading: right topic (a famous central-London landmark) but wrong place
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Paragraph 12 states the statue stands on the Victoria Embankment beside Westminster Bridge.

rewrites.newText: In the gardens outside Buckingham Palace, the royal residence
rewrites.targetsMisreading: plausible royal London location the text never gives
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Paragraph 12 locates the statue on the Victoria Embankment; Buckingham Palace is never mentioned.

rewrites.newText: Because Roman law compelled every conquered king to leave part of his kingdom to the emperor
rewrites.targetsMisreading: plausible inference the passage never licenses (legal compulsion)
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Paragraph 2 calls the bequest a voluntary gesture of loyalty; no legal requirement is stated.

rewrites.newText: It sat close to the supply base where his legions were provisioned
rewrites.targetsMisreading: invented military convenience
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Paragraph 9 says he chose the ground for its confined space and forest cover; no supply base is mentioned.

rewrites.newText: It lay near London, making it quick for his forces to reach
rewrites.targetsMisreading: detail from the wrong paragraph (London, paragraph 7)
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The final battle was in the Midlands along Watling Street, not near London.

rewrites.newText: The valley contained fresh streams that would keep his ten thousand soldiers supplied
rewrites.targetsMisreading: plausible but unsupported practical detail (water)
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Paragraph 9 attributes his choice to the confining terrain, not water supplies.

rewrites.newText: He intended to surround Boudicca's warriors from behind and cut off their retreat
rewrites.targetsMisreading: misreading of paragraph 10 (the escape was blocked by the Britons' OWN wagons)
rewrites.wrongnessReason: He chose the valley to limit how many warriors could engage; the retreat was blocked by Boudicca's own wagons.

rewrites.newText: That the archaeological evidence from Colchester is confused and cannot be trusted
rewrites.targetsMisreading: opposite of what the physical evidence shows
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The burnt destruction layer is reliable proof of a great fire, not that the archaeology is untrustworthy.

rewrites.newText: That Boudicca's warriors must have been armed with unusually advanced weapons for the period
rewrites.targetsMisreading: inference the passage never licenses from burnt debris
rewrites.wrongnessReason: A layer of burnt debris proves fire, not weapon technology.

rewrites.newText: That Colchester was originally built and settled by the Romans as a colony for soldiers
rewrites.targetsMisreading: true-in-the-text but not what the destruction layer proves
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The Romans did found Colchester, but the destruction layer proves a fire, not who built the town.

rewrites.newText: He had been outwitted and deceived by one of Boudicca's clever schemes into abandoning the city
rewrites.targetsMisreading: invented trickery not present in the text
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Paragraph 7 calls the retreat a cold but calculated decision he made himself; Boudicca did not trick him.

rewrites.newText: He was a frightened coward who fled from the approaching danger to save only his own life
rewrites.targetsMisreading: emotional negative reading the neutral facts do not support
rewrites.wrongnessReason: He ordered an organised retreat and advised civilians to leave, acting strategically rather than cowardly.

rewrites.newText: A determination to defend the ancient Celtic religion and its priests against the new beliefs the Romans were imposing
rewrites.targetsMisreading: true-of-the-era motive but unsupported by THIS text
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Paragraph 3 gives her motive as fury at being flogged, her daughters assaulted, and the kingdom seized; religion is never named.

rewrites.newText: Because the enormous bronze statue is the largest and most impressive monument anywhere in the whole of London
rewrites.targetsMisreading: over-literal focus on size instead of symbolic meaning
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Paragraph 12 makes the placement symbolic because of irony, not size.

rewrites.newText: Because the Houses of Parliament are the most visited and most photographed tourist building in the entire city of London
rewrites.targetsMisreading: irrelevant tourism fact substituted for the symbolic point
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The symbolism lies in the irony of Boudicca watching over the seat of government, not visitor numbers.

rewrites.newText: Because Boudicca is remembered and honoured there as the very first woman ever to take her seat inside the English Parliament
rewrites.targetsMisreading: factually impossible claim (she lived c. 60 AD)
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Boudicca lived around 60 AD, centuries before Parliament existed; the placement is symbolic.

rewrites.newText: Because the same craftsmen and architects who constructed the Houses of Parliament were also responsible for designing and casting the statue
rewrites.targetsMisreading: irrelevant construction connection not in the text
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The symbolism is the irony of her placement; no link between the makers is stated.

rewrites.newText: To explain in detail the archaeological methods used to excavate the destruction layer at Colchester
rewrites.targetsMisreading: one small detail mistaken for the whole purpose
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The destruction layer is a single supporting detail; the purpose is a balanced account of the rebellion.

rewrites.newText: To argue forcefully that the Roman invasion of Britain was wrong and should never have happened
rewrites.targetsMisreading: one-sided argument mistaken for a balanced account
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The author is balanced, even flagging Roman propaganda; the passage does not argue the invasion was wrong.

rewrites.newText: To explain why Roman civilisation was far more advanced and superior to the culture of the Celtic tribes
rewrites.targetsMisreading: cultural-superiority claim the passage never makes
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The passage is sympathetic to Boudicca and never argues Roman culture was superior.

rewrites.newText: It creates a peaceful and calm atmosphere that gently settles the reader before the story begins
rewrites.targetsMisreading: opposite tone to the dramatic opening
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The opening describes the empire shaken to its foundations, creating drama, not calm.

rewrites.newText: It bores the reader by opening with a dull historical date instead of any real excitement
rewrites.targetsMisreading: over-literal reaction to the opening year (60 AD)
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The date sets up a dramatic contrast between a mighty empire and one woman, designed to grip the reader.

rewrites.newText: Beside the River Thames in the south of England
rewrites.targetsMisreading: wrong famous English river
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Paragraph 2 places the estate near the River Wear in County Durham, not on the Thames.

rewrites.newText: Taking his father's greyhound for a walk
rewrites.targetsMisreading: detail from the wrong part of the story (the greyhound, paragraphs 8-10)
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Paragraph 2 says he was fishing; the greyhound belongs to the later battle plan.

rewrites.newText: Exploring a dark cave in the nearby hills
rewrites.targetsMisreading: invented setting
rewrites.wrongnessReason: He caught the creature while fishing at the river; no cave is involved.

rewrites.newText: Hunting wild animals in the forest near the estate
rewrites.targetsMisreading: plausible noble pastime but wrong activity
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Paragraph 2 shows him fishing, not hunting.

rewrites.newText: Swimming in the cold waters of the River Wear one morning
rewrites.targetsMisreading: right location (the river) but wrong activity
rewrites.wrongnessReason: He was fishing, not swimming, when the creature took his line.

rewrites.newText: The greyhound broke free and escaped from the castle before the horn signal was given
rewrites.targetsMisreading: invented escape not in the text
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Paragraph 10 says the plan failed because the father ran out before releasing the dog.

rewrites.newText: The greyhound ran faster than John's father and reached the rock first after the horn
rewrites.targetsMisreading: reversal of the actual order of arrival
rewrites.wrongnessReason: John saw his father first; the greyhound arrived only moments later.

rewrites.newText: John was unable to find his sword after the exhausting battle and could not kill the animal
rewrites.targetsMisreading: invented weapon problem
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The plan failed because the father appeared before the dog was released; John did kill the greyhound.

rewrites.newText: He had completely forgotten about the creature he once threw into the well as a boy
rewrites.targetsMisreading: opposite of the truth (he confronted it)
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Paragraph 6 says he discovered the devastation and resolved to put it right.

rewrites.newText: He had grown even more reckless and irresponsible than he had been in his wild youth
rewrites.targetsMisreading: opposite of the maturation the text describes
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Paragraph 6 describes him as older, wiser and remorseful.

rewrites.newText: His father was protected by thick armour that no ordinary weapon could pierce
rewrites.targetsMisreading: invented physical obstacle, misattributing the armour to the father
rewrites.wrongnessReason: It was John who wore the spearhead armour; he spared his father out of love.

rewrites.newText: Because the Worm returned from the river after many years and finally killed the ninth lord
rewrites.targetsMisreading: invented survival of the Worm
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The Worm was destroyed in the river; the curse ended by reaching nine generations.

rewrites.newText: Because after the ninth lord died there were no heirs left to continue the Lambton family line
rewrites.targetsMisreading: invented extinction of the family line
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The curse was fulfilled because this was the ninth generation, not because the family ran out of heirs.

rewrites.newText: Because the ninth lord was the very first Lambton to die peacefully and quietly in his own bed
rewrites.targetsMisreading: subtle inversion of the curse's terms
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The ninth lord died in his carriage; the curse ended by completing nine generations.

rewrites.newText: Because the ninth lord discovered a powerful magic spell that finally broke the ancient family curse forever
rewrites.targetsMisreading: invented magical resolution
rewrites.wrongnessReason: No spell is mentioned; the curse simply expired after nine generations.

rewrites.newText: They are genuinely frightened by the gruesome story and warn readers to take it seriously
rewrites.targetsMisreading: wrong emotion drawn from the word 'gruesome'
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Paragraph 1 is warm and amused, calling the tale delightfully gruesome; the tone is playful.

rewrites.newText: They believe the old legend is embarrassing and should quietly be forgotten by the local people
rewrites.targetsMisreading: opposite of the author's evident enjoyment
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The author celebrates the legend's endurance and relishes it.

rewrites.newText: They firmly believe that every single detail of the legend is completely true and historically accurate
rewrites.targetsMisreading: opposite of the author's stated scepticism
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Paragraph 1 says whether any of it is true is impossible to say.

rewrites.newText: It shows that the father never truly loved John and cared nothing for whether he lived or died
rewrites.targetsMisreading: opposite of what the tears of relief convey
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The father's tears and outstretched arms show overwhelming love and relief.

rewrites.newText: It creates humour because the father's clumsy and comical behaviour makes the reader laugh out loud at the whole scene
rewrites.targetsMisreading: wrong tone (comic instead of poignant)
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The moment is tender and tragic, since the father's rush triggers the curse.

rewrites.newText: Because they had already become extremely rare and needed protection from hunters
rewrites.targetsMisreading: detail from the wrong period (rarity came centuries later)
rewrites.wrongnessReason: In medieval London kites were among the most common birds; rarity came only with the later decline.

rewrites.newText: A technical scientific paper focused entirely on red kite genetics
rewrites.targetsMisreading: one detail (genetics) mistaken for the text type
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Genetics is a single supporting point; the passage is a general informative account.

rewrites.newText: A fictional adventure story about the rescue of a single bird
rewrites.targetsMisreading: factual account mistaken for fiction
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The passage reports a real, dated conservation programme with statistics.

rewrites.newText: A persuasive letter written to campaign against the practice of game shooting
rewrites.targetsMisreading: a mentioned topic mistaken for the whole form
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Game shooting is one cause of harm; the passage is a balanced informative account.

rewrites.newText: A travel guide describing places to visit across the beautiful Chiltern Hills
rewrites.targetsMisreading: the setting mistaken for the subject
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The Chilterns are the setting; the passage is about the kite's recovery.

rewrites.newText: Welsh farmers refused to protect the birds and instead drove them away from all their land
rewrites.targetsMisreading: opposite of the text (farmers were paid to tolerate them)
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Paragraph 4 says farmers were paid to tolerate the kites; the slow recovery was caused by inbreeding.

rewrites.newText: There were far too many other predators in the remote Welsh valleys that hunted and competed with them
rewrites.targetsMisreading: invented predator pressure
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The slow recovery was due to inbreeding and low fertility, not other predators.

rewrites.newText: There was simply not enough food in the remote Welsh oak valleys to support a steadily growing population
rewrites.targetsMisreading: invented food shortage
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The cause given is inbreeding and eggs failing to hatch, not a shortage of food.

rewrites.newText: The Welsh kites belonged to a completely different species that could not breed with the others
rewrites.targetsMisreading: false species distinction
rewrites.wrongnessReason: They were the same species; the issue was the small, inbred Welsh population.

rewrites.newText: Importing young kites from Sweden and Spain turned out to be far cheaper than capturing the protected Welsh birds
rewrites.targetsMisreading: invented cost motive
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The reason was the genetic health of imported birds, not cost.

rewrites.newText: That kites completely avoid all urban areas and never fly near towns or busy roads
rewrites.targetsMisreading: claim contradicted by the text
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Paragraph 10 shows kites circling over motorways; the tracking revealed long-distance travel, not avoidance of towns.

rewrites.newText: The maximum distance a kite is able to carry food back to its nest
rewrites.targetsMisreading: over-literal reading of the word 'carrying'
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Carrying capacity is an ecological term for sustainable population size, not distance.

rewrites.newText: The maximum total weight of birds and animals that the land is able to support
rewrites.targetsMisreading: literal reading of 'capacity' as physical weight
rewrites.wrongnessReason: The phrase refers to the maximum number of kites the habitat can feed, not any weight.

rewrites.newText: The amount of pollution and waste that the surrounding habitat is able to safely absorb
rewrites.targetsMisreading: invented environmental meaning
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Nothing about pollution is involved; it means the sustainable population of kites.

rewrites.newText: The total number of different bird and animal species that can live together in one shared habitat
rewrites.targetsMisreading: swaps population size of one species for species diversity
rewrites.wrongnessReason: It refers to the maximum number of kites (a single species), not how many species can coexist.

rewrites.newText: Illegal poisoning by gamekeepers on shooting estates
rewrites.targetsMisreading: correct answer here is the one NOT mentioned; this stays a genuinely mentioned threat
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Illegal poisoning IS named as a threat in paragraph 11, so it is not the 'NOT mentioned' answer.

rewrites.newText: Fatal collisions with vehicles on busy roads and motorways
rewrites.targetsMisreading: expanded but still a real, mentioned threat
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Paragraph 11 names vehicle collisions as a cause of death, so this is genuinely mentioned.

rewrites.newText: Growing competition for food with the expanding buzzard population
rewrites.targetsMisreading: expanded but still a real, mentioned threat
rewrites.wrongnessReason: Paragraph 11 states kites compete for food with buzzards, so this is mentioned.
